segments.s625-g113: وزانجا is a fused literary form meaning و از آنجا (“and/from there”), a locative adverbial introducing origin. خرامان is a manner adverb (from خرامیدن) placed before the verb to mean ‘stately/with a proud gait’. بیامد is the classical past 3rd-person singular (‘he came’), a literary form equivalent to آمد. بدر here is a proper name (not the noun “full moon”), marking the destination/place/person reached.
segments.s625-g114: خرد یافته is a compound adjectival phrase: خرد (wisdom) + یافته (acquired/found), literally ‘having acquired wisdom’ and so functioning as a pre-nominal modifier. موبد is the head noun (mōbed, a Zoroastrian priest). پرهنر is a postpositive adjective (‘skilled, cultured’) that further qualifies موبد. Together they mean ‘the mobed, who had acquired wisdom and was skilled.’
segments.s625-g115: فرستاده is the noun ‘messenger/envoy’ (literally ‘sent one’) and serves as the sentence subject. قیصر is the title/name ‘Caesar’ in a genitive relation (envoy of Caesar). نامدار is a postpositive adjective meaning ‘renowned’ modifying قیصر (or the whole title), hence ‘the renowned Caesar’s envoy.’
segments.s625-g116: سوی خانه رفت is a straightforward clause: سوی (‘toward/to’) + خانه (‘the house’) + رفت (past tense ‘he went’). از بر شهریار is an idiomatic locative complement: از (= ‘from’) + بر here in the sense used in classical Persian with از to mean ‘from the presence of/away from’ and شهریار (‘the king/shah’). Put together: ‘he left the shah’s presence and went toward the house.’
segments.s625-g57: چنین گفت موبد به فرزانه مرد is a poetic word order: insbesondere the verb گفت (said) comes immediately after manner adverb چنین (thus), followed by the subject موبد (the mobad/priest) and the indirect object phrase به فرزانه مرد (to the wise man). به is the preposition 'to', and فرزانه modifies مرد ('wise man'); the whole segment introduces reported speech.
segments.s625-g58: که introduces the content of what was said ('that' / introduces the quotation). مشتاتب is an imperative form 'mashtab' = 'hurry!' and functions as the first command. وز here is an archaic prepositional form equivalent to از ('from'), introducing راه دانش 'the path of knowledge'; مگرد is a negative imperative 'do not turn/withdraw' (literary), so the two imperatives form the mobad's counsel: 'Hurry, and do not turn away from the way of knowledge.'
segments.s625-g59: مر is an archaic dative 'to me' (marks the experiencer). این را که گفتی تو = 'that/this which you said' (این را with the object marker را topicalizes 'this', followed by the relative clause که گفتی تو 'that you said'). پاسخ یکیست is the predicate: پاسخ 'answer' + یکیست 'is one', i.e. 'the answer is one'. Word order places the topicalized object before the copular clause.
segments.s625-g60: سخن is the subject 'the matter / speech'. در درون و برون is an adverbial phrase 'inwardly and outwardly' built with the preposition در 'in' plus the paired nouns درون 'inside' and برون 'outside'. اندکیست is the fused predicate (اندکی + ست) meaning 'is little/insignificant'. The clause states that both inside and outside the matter is slight.
segments.s625-g61: برون آسمان places the locative/adverbial word برون 'outside' before the noun آسمان 'sky', giving an inverted predication 'outside (is) the sky'. و connects the clauses; درونش is درون 'inside' with the enclitic -ش 'its', and هواست is هوا + ست (copula) 'is air'. The clause contrasts exterior (sky) with interior (air).
segments.s625-g62: زبر here functions adverbially as 'above / on high'. فر یزدان is a nominal phrase 'the divine glory/majesty (of God)' with فر 'glory' followed by یزدان 'God'. فرمانرواست is a fused predicate meaning 'is sovereign / rules'. The clause therefore says that above (all) is God's glorious rule.
segments.s625-g63: همان is an emphatic demonstrative 'that very / the same'. بیکران is an adjective 'boundless' modifying the implied subject. در جهان 'in the world' is a locative phrase, and ایزدست is the predicate formed from ایزد (God) + ست (copula) meaning 'is God' or 'it is God'. The overall clause identifies the boundless being in the world as God.
segments.s625-g64: اگر is the conditional 'if'. تاب گیری is a verbal phrase where تاب 'brightness/radiance' or 'glory' is the object and گیری (from گرفتن) is 'you take/obtain' (second-person form in a literary conditional: 'if you obtain'). به دانش بدست is an instrumental phrase 'by/through knowledge' (به + دانش) with بدست used adverbially 'by means of / through'. The whole conditional means 'if you can gain radiance by knowledge'.
segments.s625-g89: همی is the habitual/continuative particle placed before the verb to indicate ongoing or customary action. خوار is an adjective meaning 'despised/low' used as the quality applied to someone; گیری is the present stem/infinitive-form of گرفتن used here to indicate 'take/make' (i.e. 'make low' or 'treat as low'); شمار is the noun 'count/reckoning' used as the object/theme, and ورا is the enclitic pronoun 'to him/it' referring back to the person affected.
segments.s625-g90: همان is a demonstrative 'that/the same' qualifying the following noun. گردش روزگار is a genitive phrase 'the turning/circuit (گردش) of time/fate (روزگار)'; ورا again is the enclitic 'to him/it', so the whole phrase means 'that very turn (of) fate for him'. Word order is demonstrative + noun + genitive + pronoun.
segments.s625-g91: کسی here means 'someone/anyone'; the particle کو is an archaic relative/connecting particle (used like 'who/whom' or 'that'), not the modern interrogative 'where' (no matching modern sense is supplied, so we mark it as unmatched). ببیند is the present tense (habitual/episodic) of 'to see' (he/one sees), ز is the poetic preposition 'from' or 'by', and پرتاب تیر is the noun phrase 'the throwing/launching of an arrow' (پرتاب = throw/launch, تیر = arrow).
segments.s625-g92: بماند is the verb 'remains/is left' used here to state a result or state that endures. شگفت functions as a noun/adjective 'a wonder/astonishment' (i.e. 'one is astonished'); اندرو (اند + رو) is an enclitic/locative 'in it/therein'; تیز is an attributive adjective 'swift/sudden' (here describing the following noun); ویر is the noun 'ruin/destruction'. Together: 'therein remains a quick/sudden ruin.'
segments.s625-g93: ستاره is 'star' (celestial) as subject; همی again marks habitual/continuative action; بشمرد is the present/future-like form of 'to count/reckon' (he counts); ز آسمان is the prepositional phrase 'from the sky/heaven' indicating source ('counts from the heavens').
segments.s625-g94: ازین is از + این ('from/than this' or 'of this'); here it means 'than this' or 'from this' with ین as the orthographic variant of این. خوارتر is the comparative 'more despicable/more lowly'. چیست‌ای is a rhetorical/questioning 'what is... O...', where ای is the vocative marker 'O' attached to the following noun. شادمان is a vocative address 'O joyful one' (a noun/adjective used here to address someone).
segments.s625-g95: من این دانم is a straightforward clause 'I know this' (من subject, این object, دانم 'I know'). ار هست is the conditional 'if there is' (ار = if, هست = is/exist) and پاسخ جزین means 'an answer other than this' (پاسخ = answer, جزین = except this/'other than this'). The phrase sets up a conditional: 'I know this: if there is an answer besides this...'
segments.s625-g96: فراخست is an archaic/descriptive predicate meaning 'was/is wide/expansive' (here used to mean 'is broad'); رای is 'opinion/plan/counsel' and جهان‌آفرین is a compound epithet 'world‑creator' (i.e. God). Together they state the explanatory clause: 'the Creator's counsel is broad.' Note the older -ست ending in فراخست.
segments.s625-g49: به موبد: a prepositional phrase 'to the mobad' (به + indirect object). چنین گفت: 'thus said' — گفت is the past-tense verb 'said'; the word order places the indirect object and the adverbial before the verb. کای رهنمون: a vocative 'کای' (O) addressing the mobad, followed by رهنمون 'guide'.
segments.s625-g50: This is a relative question clause: چه چیز 'what thing' + آنک 'that/which', followed by the verb phrase خوانی همی 'you read/recite (now/habitually)'. همی is an archaic/prosodic particle marking ongoing or habitual action; اندرون 'within/inside' is the locative complement placed after the verb phrase.
segments.s625-g51: دگر 'the other/another' introduces a second item; آنک 'which' begins a relative clause. بیرونش 'outside him/it' combines بیرون 'outside' with the enclitic -ش 'his/it', and خوانی همی is again the present/ habitual 'you recite/call' construction (خوانی + همی).
segments.s625-g52: جزین نیز 'besides/except this also' frames the comment; نامش 'his name' is a bound noun + clitic object. ندانی همی is the archaic present-negative form 'you do not know' (ندانی = n + دانى, 2nd-person present), with همی adding progressive/habitual sense.
segments.s625-g53: زبر چیست‌ای مهتر: زبر 'above/upper' followed by the copula-question چیست (what is), with the vocative ای مهتر 'O chief' attached immediately after. The second clause و زیر چیست parallels the first: 'and what is زیر (below)?' — classic poetic inversion and parallelism with the copula.
segments.s625-g54: همان بیکرانه چه: همان 'that' + adjective بیکرانه 'boundless/immeasurable' followed by the question particle چه 'what/which (is)'. و خوار کیست: a parallel question 'and who is the despicable/lowly?' (کی 'who' + ست 'is' as copula).
segments.s625-g55: چه چیز 'what thing' + آنک 'that which' introduces a relative clause; نامش 'his name' (name + clitic) with فراوان بود 'was widespread/abundant' — فراوان modifies 'name' and بود is the past-tense copula 'was'.
segments.s625-g56: مر او را 'to me him' (مر = 'me/to me' placed for emphasis) + را marks او as definite object; به هر جای 'in every place/region' is a locative phrase; فرمان بود is an idiomatic pereiphrasis 'there was command' = 'he had command/authority'. Together: 'He had authority over me everywhere.'
segments.s625-g17: سخن هرچ گویی تو — a relative/nominal clause: سخن هرچ (literally “speech whatever”) = “whatever (words)”; گویی is the verb 'you say' placed after the phrase (poetic order) and تو is the subject pronoun. پَاسخ دهیم follows as the main clause: دهیم is 1st‑pl present/subjunctive used here with an optative/intentive sense (“we will give/let us give” or simply “we give/answer”).
segments.s625-g18: وز آواز تو روز فرخ نهیم — وز is a poetic/contracted connective (here linking clauses; functionally like و/از or “and/by”), آواز تو = “your voice” (genitive), روز فرخ = “fortunate day” (noun + adjective), and نهیم is the 1st‑pl present/subjunctive of نهادن “to place/set” used hortatively/intentively (“we set/make”). The whole means “and by your voice we will make (set) a happy day.”
segments.s625-g19: فرستاده پیر کرد آفرین — subject + verb + object: فرستاده پیر 'the old messenger/elder envoy' is the subject, کرد is the past simple of کردن 'made/did', and آفرین is the noun/exclamation 'praise/cry of approval' (here an acclamation he utters). Word order is typical poetic flexibility but functions as 'the old messenger made/uttered a cry of praise.'
segments.s625-g20: که بی‌تو مبادا زمان و زمین — که introduces a subordinate optative/wish clause ('that/so that' → here 'that/may it be that'); بی‌تو = 'without you'; مبادا is the negative optative/imperative formula meaning 'may it not be' or 'let there not be'; زمان و زمین = 'time and earth' (i.e. 'time and the world'). Together: 'that without you may time and earth not be.'
segments.s625-g21: هران پادشاهی که دارد خرد — هران (هر آن) = 'each of those' or 'every', پادشاهی here = 'rulership/ruler' in context (poetic use), که introduces the relative clause, دارد خرد = 'has wisdom' with دارد as the present/stative auxiliary 'has/possesses' and خرد 'wisdom'. The clause means 'each king/ruler who has wisdom.'
segments.s625-g22: ز گفت خردمند رامش برد — ز (poetic preposition = 'from/by') + گفت ('words/speech') + خردمند ('the wise') forms an ablative/agent phrase 'from the words of the wise'; رامش is 'tranquility/peace', and برد is the past verb 'took/gained' (here 'gained peace'). Word order places the source phrase first for emphasis: 'from the words of the wise he gained tranquility.'
segments.s625-g23: به یزدان خردمند نزدیک‌تر — comparative construction with به + dative: به یزدان 'to/with God', خردمند modifies یزدان 'the wise God', and نزدیک‌تر is the comparative adjective 'closer'. This phrase sets the comparative frame: 'the closer (one is) to the wise God...' (poetic omission of an explicit subject).
segments.s625-g24: بداندیش را روز تاریک‌تر — بداندیش 'the ill‑wisher' is marked by را as a topical/emphatic marker here (topic fronting), روز 'day' and تاریک‌تر 'darker' form the predication: 'for the ill‑wisher the day is darker' or more smoothly 'the ill‑wisher's day is darker.' The clause completes the comparative sense begun before.
segments.s625-g25: This clause states a comparison: تو (you) followed by بر + noun to mean 'above/superior to' (بر here = 'over/against'), then مهتران جهان 'the chiefs of the world'. Note the order: subject (تو) + comparative preposition (بر) + plural noun phrase.
segments.s625-g26: که introduces an explanatory/relative clause ('for/that' or 'indeed'), listing roles and superiority: هم ... و ... و هم ... ی — 'both ... and ... and also ... is'. The final ی is an archaic/copulative clitic (from هست) marking existence/being ('is'). The enumerated nouns (مهتر, شاه, بهتر) are linked by و and emphasized by هم.
segments.s625-g27: ترا here is the dative/possessive expression 'to you' (written as the clitic ت + the object/definite marker را), so the phrase says 'to you is/are ...'. It then lists qualities: دانش (knowledge), هوش (intellect/wit), دادست (an archaic fused form meaning 'there is justice' or 'justice is [with you]') and فر (farr, royal glory).
segments.s625-g28: بر آیین شاهان پیروزگر qualifies the preceding list: بر + آیین = 'over/upon/regarding the practice/ways', شاهان 'kings' and پیروزگر as an adjective 'triumphant' or 'victorious'. The whole phrase means these gifts place you above the rites/customs of victorious kings.
segments.s625-g29: همانت (that very) + خرد (wisdom) + هست (is) states 'that same wisdom is [with you]'. The phrase continues with و پاکیزه رای 'and a pure judgment'; پاکیزه is an adjective modifying رای (opinion/judgment). Word order follows classical Persian: demonstrative/emphatic + noun + copula.
segments.s625-g30: This short clause gives the social ranking: بر + هوشمندان = 'over/above the wise' or 'as for the wise', توی = 'among', and کدخدای = 'headman' (used metaphorically: 'you are head among the wise'). Note the inverted classical order where the prepositional phrase frames the role.
segments.s625-g31: که here introduces a wish/optative clause ('may...'). جاوید + بادی is an archaic optative construction: جاوید 'everlasting' followed by بادی (here functioning as an optative particle 'may you be', archaic) and then تن و جان 'body and soul', with درست 'sound/proper' qualifying them. Grammatically this is a blessing: 'may your body and soul be eternal and sound.'
segments.s625-g32: مبیناد is the archaic subjunctive/optative form meaning 'may (it) show/appear' (here 'may [the heavens] not make you appear weak'); گردون 'the sky/heaven' is the subject, میان 'among' indicates the group, تو 'you', and سست 'weak'. The sequence is classical word order where verb-like مبیناد follows its noun arguments.
segments.s625-g97: سخن‌دان قیصر is a noun phrase 'the emperor/Caesar's spokesman' (noun + possessor). چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. پاسخ شنید is a past-tense clause: پرساق (پاسخ) precedes the verb شنید (3rd person singular past), giving the usual SOV/verb-final order of literary Persian.
segments.s625-g98: زمین را is the definite direct object marked by را and is the object of ببوسید ('he kissed'). The conjunction و links two coordinated past verbs (ببوسید و گزید) that share the same subject; فرمان is the (direct) object of گزید. گزید here means 'chose/issued' (to select/establish a command).
segments.s625-g99: به بهرام گفت places the indirect object ('to Bahram') before the verb گفت (past 3rd singular 'he said'). The vocative ای جهاندار شاه follows as direct address: 'O sovereign king.' This is a typical sequence introducing direct speech in literary Persian.
segments.s625-g100: ز یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'from/of God' (here meaning 'over or with respect to the divine'). برین‌بر فزونی is a poetic locution meaning 'superiority/being above' (برین‌بر = 'upon/above this'), with فزونی 'increase/excess' as the abstract noun. مخواه is a negative imperative/prohibitive (2nd-person singular) meaning 'do not desire/seek'.
segments.s625-g101: که introduces an explanatory clause ('for' / 'that'). گیتی سراسر is a noun + postpositive adjective 'the entire world' (poetic order). به فرمان uses به as an instrumental/prepositional marker 'by/through the command', and تست here is the archaic copular/possessive form meaning 'is yours' (2nd-person singular).
segments.s625-g102: سر سرکشان literally 'the heads of the rebels/recalcitrant ones' (سر = head). زیر پیمان means 'under the covenant/treaty' where زیر conveys subjection ('subject to'). تست again is the archaic 'is yours' copula referring back to the addressee; word order places the predicate-final verb after its nominal complements.
segments.s625-g103: پسند بزرگان فرخ‌نژاد is a nominal phrase 'the approval/esteem of the great, the well-born'; بزرگان فرخ‌نژاد is a genitive-like grouping 'the great ones of noble birth' (modifier following the head in literary style).
segments.s625-g104: ندارد جهان is the negative predicate 'the world does not have' (ندارد = does not possess/exist for). چون تو شاهی is a comparative phrase 'a king like you' (چون = like + pronoun + noun), and به یاد is an adverbial phrase 'in memory/record' (literally 'to memory'), so together meaning 'the world has no memory/record of a king like you.'
segments.s625-g41: بدو (a prepositional/indirect-object clitic) precedes the verb and marks the recipient: “to him.” گفت is the past tense verb “said” (here used with an omitted quoted command). شاه is the subject “the king” placed after the verb (typical older/Persian word order: verb + subject). این سخنها is the quoted object phrase “these words,” and بگوی is an imperative/jussive (archaisms of verbal morphology) meaning “say” (introducing what is to be uttered).
segments.s625-g42: سخن‌گوی را uses را as the definite/object marker (the speaker as a marked object). بیشتر modifies the following noun and is comparative “more.” آب‌روی is a noun meaning “reputation / honor.” The whole noun phrase gives what is to be given to the speaker: “(give) the speaker more reputation,” with the verb implied from the earlier command.
segments.s625-g43: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past tense verb meaning “he commanded/said.” تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result (“so that / that …”). موبد موبدان is an appositional/title phrase: the single noun موبد (a priest) followed by موبدان (the plural/title ‘mōbeds’), functioning as ‘mobed of the mobeds’ — i.e., chief priest.
segments.s625-g44: بشد here is the clause verb meaning “become / be made” (subordinate/jussive sense governed by تا). پیش with با introduces the setting “in the presence of / before,” with با marking accompaniment (“with”). مهتران و ردان are plural nouns naming the groups present (“the elders/chiefs and the notables/others”); their exact glosses are cultural/archaic titles, so they function as the object of the prepositional phrase “in the presence of ….”
segments.s625-g45: بشد موبد is a result/passive-style clause: “the mobed was made” / “he became mobed.” و connects clauses. هرکه is the indefinite relative “whoever.” دانا is an adjective “learned/wise” modifying the whoever. بدند is a past/plural verbal form with an omitted agent (they — i.e., the king or those present) meaning “they made/appointed” or “they were made” depending on context; here it indicates the appointing action by others.
segments.s625-g46: به marks the domain/role (“in / with respect to”). هر دانشی means “every kind of knowledge” (هر = every; دانشی = knowledge/branch of learning). بر is reinforcing the domain (“in / with regard to”). توانا is a predicate/adjective “capable / able.” بدند again is the past/plural verb with omitted agent: “they made (him) capable.” The clause literally says “for every knowledge they made (him) able.”
segments.s625-g47: سخن‌گوی is the subject “the speaker.” بگشاد is an archaic past/transitive verb meaning “unveiled / opened up / revealed.” راز is the object “the secret.” از نهفت is a two-word phrase where از marks source (“from / out of”) and نهفت is an adjective/noun “that which was hidden / concealment,” so together “revealed the secret from concealment.”
segments.s625-g48: سخنهای قیصر is a genitive phrase “the words/speeches of the Caesar/emperor” placed before the verb. به موبد marks the indirect object with به (“to the mobed”). بگفت is the past tense “he said / he told,” so the clause reads “(he) told the mobed the emperor’s words.” Word order is typical older Persian: [object/genitive] + [indirect object] + verb.
segments.s625-g105: همان نیز is a fronting/emphatic phrase meaning 'moreover/that also'; دستورت is the noun دستور with the enclitic -ت 'your', so 'your instruction/command'; از موبدان is a prepositional phrase 'from the mobeds' (موبدان is the plural of موبد, a Zoroastrian priest). The whole chunk establishes the possession and source for what will be compared.
segments.s625-g106: به دانش فزونست is a comparative frame: به + noun ('in/regarding knowledge') plus فزونست (archaic fused form of 'فزون است', 'is greater/more'). فزونست is present/predicative. The trailing از بخردان uses از as the comparative 'than' and بخردان is the plural/old-form of 'the wise', so 'greater in learning than the wise.'
segments.s625-g107: همه فیلسوفان is the subject 'all the philosophers'. ورا is an old unstressed form meaning 'to him' / 'toward him' (indirect object), and بنده‌اند is a present plural copula form meaning 'they are servants' or 'they are devoted'. Together: 'All philosophers are his servants.'
segments.s625-g108: به دانایی او uses به as an agental/causal marker here ('by/through his wisdom' or 'in the face of his wisdom'); دانایی means 'wisdom'; او is 'his'; سرافگنده‌اند (present plural) means 'they are ashamed/abashed'. The clause says that, because of his wisdom, they are put to shame.
segments.s625-g109: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; بهرام is the proper name; بشنید is the past tense 'he heard'; شادی نمود is a light verb construction where نمود ('showed/did') takes شادی ('joy') as its object, producing 'he showed joy' → 'he rejoiced'.
segments.s625-g110: به دلش اندرون is a locative phrase 'into/within his heart' (دل + possessive clitic ش = 'his heart' and اندرون = 'inner part/within'). روشنایی فزود places the noun 'brightness/light' before the archaic past verb فزود ('increased'); together: 'brightness increased within his heart.'
segments.s625-g111: به موبدم is 'to my mobed' (به + موبد with clitic -م 'my'); درم is the coin 'dirham' as the object; داد is the past 'gave'. بدره is a name/epithet (literally 'full moon' in poetry) and نیز 'also' links to the next list of gifts — so this clause reports the giving of money and sets up further gifts.
segments.s625-g112: همان جامه و اسپ و بسیار چیز is a noun list: همان 'the same' (referring back), جامه 'robe/garment', اسپ an archaic word for 'steed/horse', and بسیار چیز 'many things'. The phrase enumerates the other gifts given (the same robe and horse and many items).
segments.s625-g65: زبر ('above') + چون + بهشتست forms a simile: 'زبر چون بهشتست' = 'above is like heaven'. Persian places the copula (بهشتست, 'is heaven/heavenly') at the end; چون ('like/as') precedes the noun. The conjunction و connects the clause to 'دوزخ به زیر' where 'دوزخ' (hell) is paired with the prepositional phrase 'به زیر' ('beneath/under'), literally 'hell below.'
segments.s625-g66: بد آن را is an exclamatory, poetic construction: 'بد' (bad/woe) precedes 'آن را' ('that one' with را used for topical/emphatic force), yielding 'woe to that one'. که introduces the relative clause describing 'that one': 'که باشد به یزدان دلیر' — 'who is/should be bold toward God.' باشد here is an optative/subjunctive/3rd-person form used in classical Persian to characterize the person, and به یزدان دلیر means 'bold toward/against God' (i.e., defying God).
segments.s625-g67: دگر ('another') introduces a different person; آنک is an older relative pronoun ('who/that') linking to the following predicate. بسیار نامش بود is a predicative phrase: بسیار ('great/much') modifies نامش ('his name/fame') and بود is the past copula 'was' — together 'whose fame was great.'
segments.s625-g68: رونده is a participial/attributive form meaning 'one who goes/roams' (subject), followed by the adverbial prepositional phrase به هر جای ('to every place/wherever') and the result phrase کامش بود ('his desire was' / 'his wish was fulfilled'). The verb بود again functions as the copula linking the participial subject to the result.
segments.s625-g69: خرد دارد is a compact clause meaning '(one) has wisdom' or 'wisdom exists/one possesses wisdom' (دارَد is the present/ever-present 'has'). The vocative ای پیر ('O elder') addresses someone directly and is inserted before the qualifier بسیار نام ('much/great renown'), which comments on the result or consequence of having wisdom.
segments.s625-g70: رساند is the verb 'brings/causes' with خرد ('wisdom') as its agent (subject). پادشا را is the definite object ('the king') marked by را, and به کام ('to [his] desire/satisfaction') expresses the result. The whole clause means 'wisdom brings the king to (his) desire' or 'wisdom causes the king's wish to be fulfilled.'
segments.s625-g71: یکی ... خوانند is an impersonal/classical-present construction: 'one they call Mehr' (یکی = 'one, a certain one'); خوانند is the archaic 3rd-person plural present 'they call'. و دیگر وفا parallels the first clause: 'and another (they call) Vafa.' Here مهر and وفا function as names/titles.
segments.s625-g72: خرد دور شد is a simple past/resultative clause: 'wisdom became distant' / 'wisdom departed' (دور 'distant', شد 'became'). درد ماند و جفا uses ماند ('remained') as the verb with درد ('sorrow/affliction') and جفا ('cruelty/injustice') as the things left behind: 'pain remained and cruelty.'
segments.s625-g1: This is a simple past clause in S (subject) O V order: سپهبد (subject) + فرستاده را (definite object marked by را) + پیش خواند (verb phrase). پیش before the verb here has the sense 'into the presence of / forward', and خواند is the past 3rd‑person singular of the verb meaning 'to call/summon'.
segments.s625-g2: بران نامور functions as a nominal phrase 'that famed one' (بران as a demonstrative/marker referring back to the person), followed by پیشگاهش 'his presence/at court' (پیشگاه + enclitic -ش = his). نشاند is an archaic/poetic past form meaning 'caused to sit / seated' (used transitively here).
segments.s625-g3: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'; بشنید is past 3rd‑person 'he heard'. The actual subject follows the verb in inverted order: بیدار شاه جهان 'the alert (vigilant) king of the world' — an epithet (بیدار) plus the title (شاه جهان).
segments.s625-g4: This repeats the action with object marking: فرستاده را (the messenger, definite object) + خواند (past 'called/summoned'). پیش مهان is a prepositional/locative phrase 'in the presence of the guests/nobles' (پیش = 'before/in the sight of', مهان = 'guests/visitors').
segments.s625-g5: The verb بیامد 'came' appears first (past 3rd‑person), followed by a string of epithets that constitute the subject: جهاندیده دانای پیر 'worldly‑wise, learned elder'. This verb‑first order is common in epic/poetic Persian to announce arrival.
segments.s625-g6: A sequence of attributive nouns/adjectives joined by و: سخن‌گوی 'eloquent/speaker', بادانش 'with‑knowledge / learned' (here functioning as 'knowledgeable'), و یادگیر 'and learner/memorizer'. These are appositive epithets further describing the man.
segments.s625-g7: This is a participial/absolute construction describing manner: به + کش + کرده (کش‌کرده = 'having drawn/stretched') with دست as its object: 'having stretched his hand'. Then two linked adjectives سرافگنده و پست 'ashamed/humiliated and lowly' describe his demeanour. The cluster modifies the main verb in the next segment.
segments.s625-g8: بر تخت شاهی is a locative phrase 'upon the royal throne' (بر = 'on/upon', تخت = 'throne', شاهی = 'royal/kingship'). به زانو نشست is an idiomatic verbal phrase 'sat down on (his) knees / knelt' — به + body part + نشست expresses posture 'sat/knelt with the [body part]'. نشست is the past of 'to sit'.
segments.s625-g73: زبان‌آوری is a nominal compound meaning 'eloquence' or 'gift of tongue' and functions as the direct object/topic here. راستی خواندش is built from خواند (past 3rd‑singular 'he called') plus the enclitic object pronoun ‑ش ('it/him'), so the phrase literally says 'he called it
segments.s625-g74: بلنداختری (an inflected form of بلنداختر) is a poetic epithet 'the high‑rising star' or 'eminent one' and here acts as the topic/subject of the next clause. زیرکی is a noun 'shrewdness/cunning', and داندش is داند (present 3rd‑singular 'he knows') plus the enclitic ‑ش ('it/him'), so 'he knows it' — i.e. 'he knows the shrewdness of the high‑rising star.' Note that classical poetry mixes past (خواند) and present (داند) forms for stylistic effect; the enclitic object points back to the preceding noun.
segments.s625-g75: گهی is an adverb meaning 'sometimes' and is repeated for contrastive effect; بردبار is an adjective 'patient/forbearing'; و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'; the second گهی again 'sometimes'; رازدار is an adjective/noun 'secret‑keeper/confidant.' The phrase lists alternating qualities: 'sometimes X and sometimes Y.'
segments.s625-g76: که introduces a result/relative clause here ('such that' / 'so that') and is sense 'that/who.' باشد is the 3rd‑singular present/subjunctive form meaning 'is/shall be' but in this context functions as 'remains' (i.e. a stative result). سخن 'speech/words' is the subject, نزد او 'in his presence' uses نزد in the sense 'in the presence of,' and پایدار is an adjective 'lasting/steady.' Together: 'so that speech remains steady in his presence.'
segments.s625-g77: پراگنده is an adjective 'widespread/scattered' describing the following noun. اینست (this is) functions as the copula 'is' (idiomatically 'this is'), and نام خرد is the noun phrase 'the name/reputation of wisdom.' The order (adjective + اینست + noun) is a poetic arrangement equivalent to 'the name of wisdom is widespread.'
segments.s625-g78: از here introduces a point of comparison/limit ('from/beyond'); اندازه‌ها means 'measures/degrees/limits'; نام او is 'his name/reputation'; بگذرد is the 3rd‑singular present/subjunctive of گذشتن and in the construction از ... بگذرد means 'to pass beyond / to exceed.' So literally 'his name passes beyond measures' = 'his renown exceeds all bounds.'
segments.s625-g79: تو 'you' is the addressee. چیزی 'anything/something' is the object of the following verb. مدان is an archaic/poetic negative imperative form (compare مَدان) meaning 'do not suppose/think/assume' (here used idiomatically), so تو چیزی مدان کز خرد برترست = 'Do not imagine there is anything that is above wisdom.' کز is the relative 'that/which' introducing the subordinate clause, and برترست is a predicative adjective 'is superior/above.'
segments.s625-g80: خرد is the noun 'wisdom.' بر is the preposition 'over/above' and here marks the domain in which wisdom rules. همه 'all/every' modifies نیکویها ('the good/excellent things' or 'virtues'). سرست is a poetic predicate meaning 'is head/chief' (سر = head), so the clause says 'wisdom is chief over all good things' or more idiomatically 'wisdom is foremost among all virtues.'
segments.s625-g33: زبانت is زبان + ـت (your tongue/your speech); ترازوست is ترازو + است with the verb contracted (poetic), so the clause literally says 'your tongue is a scale.' و simply links the clauses. گفتن is the verbal noun 'speaking/saying' used as a noun phrase, and گهر names what that speaking is being compared to ('a jewel'). Word order is subject — predicate (زبانت ترازوست) then a coordinated nominal phrase (و گفتن گهر).
segments.s625-g34: گهر سخته = گهر + (سخت) + ـه (poetic copula omitted) — an adjectival clause meaning 'a jewel is hard/rare'; سخته is the older compressed form of 'سخت است'. هرگز is an adverb ('ever/never', here intensifying rarity). که introduces a relative/content clause ('that'), بیند is classical present/optative sense of 'see' (here 'be seen / be matched'), and به زر is a prepositional phrase 'by/with gold.' The adjective follows the noun (noun + adjective), and the clause says it is difficult for a gem ever to be matched by gold.
segments.s625-g35: اگر چه here functions as a concessive 'although' (compound of اگر + چه). فرستاده is the noun 'messenger' and قیصرم is قیصر + ـم (my/of Caesar) indicating 'I am Caesar's envoy' with the first-person verb copula implied rather than expressed; the clause is therefore concessive ('although I am Caesar's envoy').
segments.s625-g36: همان 'that very' or 'the same' + چاکر 'servant/retainer' — an emphatic self-description. شاه را uses the object/topic marker را to mark 'the king' as the reference; چاکرم is چاکر + ـم (I am [a] servant), the first-person predicative form. The repetition (چاکر ... چاکرم) is formulaic/emphatic: 'I remain/ am indeed the king's servant.'
segments.s625-g37: درودی is a noun 'a greeting'; رسانم is a first-person present/past-form used in narrative meaning 'I deliver/convey' (the final ـم marks 1st person). ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (instead of از), قیصر is the source, به marks the recipient 'to', and شاه is 'the king'. The object (درودی) comes before the verb, which is common in Persian.
segments.s625-g38: که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. جاوید is an adjective 'eternal'; باد is the optative particle here ('may' — a fossilized subjunctive/imperative form used to wish something); این determines the following nouns ('this'); سر, تاج, گاه are the coordinated objects ('head, crown, and seat/throne'). Word order places the optative particle after the adjective (جاوید باد = 'may ... be eternal').
segments.s625-g39: و دیگر 'and furthermore'; که فرمود is a relative/content clause introduced by که with فرمود (past, honorific/epic register of 'said/commanded') — 'that he commanded/said'. تا introduces purpose/result here ('so that/in order that'), leading into what should be done, and هفت چیز (seven things) is the object of that ordering. The clause is reporting an order.
segments.s625-g40: بپرسم is the present-subjunctive/optative first-person form of پرسیدن with the prefix بـ marking the subjunctive in classical Persian — 'that I should ask / I am to ask.' ز is the poetic 'from/of', دانندگان is the plural noun 'the knowledgeable/experts', تو is the second-person possessive/topic 'your', and نیز 'also/too' adds that they too are to be questioned. The whole is the dependent clause of فرمود/تا: 'that I ask of your learned men as well.'
segments.s625-g81: خرد (subject) + جوید (present 3rd-sg verb) is a simple S–V clause 'wisdom seeks.' آگنده modifies راز (poetic word order places آگنده before راز) and together آگنده راز جهان functions as the object phrase 'the world's abundant/filled secrets' (the ezāfe linking is omitted by poetic economy).
segments.s625-g82: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause modifying 'راز جهان'; چشم سر is an idiom 'the eye of the head' (ordinary sight). نبیند is the negative present 3rd-sg ('does not see') and نهان is a predicate/adjectival complement 'hidden.' Word order is Subject (چشم سر ما) + Verb (نبیند) + Predicate (نهان).
segments.s625-g83: دگر marks contrast ('another' / 'on the other hand'); آنک is the relative 'who/whom.' دارد is present 3rd-sg 'holds/has.' Poetic inversion places the agent (جهاندار, 'the world-ruler') and the predicate خوار ('despised') after the relative, so the phrase reads 'the one whom the ruler holds despised.'
segments.s625-g84: به هر دانش is a prepositional phrase 'in/with every knowledge'; از introduces source/concern ('concerning/of') and connects to کرده کردگار, where کرده is used as the noun 'deed(s)' and کردگار 'the Maker/Creator.' The ezāfe is omitted for poetic brevity: 'every knowledge concerning the deeds of the Creator.'
segments.s625-g85: ستاره‌ست is a fused copular form 'is a star' (stressed predicate). رخشان is the adjective 'radiant/gleaming.' ز is the poetic variant of از ('from'), and چرخ بلند is the genitive phrase 'the high heaven(s).' Word order places the verb/copis first for poetic emphasis.
segments.s625-g86: The first که introduces a subordinate/relative clause. بینا ('sighted' or 'one who sees') is the adv./adjunct describing the knower; شمارش ('the counting' or 'reckoning') is the object; بداند is present 3rd-sg 'knows.' The second که introduces an indirect question ('how many' — چند).
segments.s625-g87: بلند آسمان را topicalizes 'the lofty sky/heavens' with را (here functioning as topic/emphasis). که introduces the following clause 'فرسنگ نیست' meaning 'there is no (measured) farsang' — i.e., no earthly measure/distance reaches it; the negative نیست follows to assert absence.
segments.s625-g88: کسی را with را topicalizes 'no one' (here in the sense 'no one' because of the negation). بدو = 'to it' (referring back to the sky); راه 'way/path' and آهنگ (here 'course/approach, pace') form a coordinate pair 'way or access.' نیست negates existence: 'no one has a way or approach to it.'
segments.s625-g9: بپرسید is a third-person past narrative form 'asked' (ب‑ prefix is the storytelling/narrative marker). The following proper name بهرام is the agent; و is the coordinating conjunction 'and/then'. بنواختش is a past verb 'struck/beat' (بنواخت) with the enclitic pronoun ‑ش 'him/it' attached as object.
segments.s625-g10: بر is the preposition 'upon/on', تخت is 'throne', and پیروزه here is a poetic adjective 'victorious' modifying تخت. بنشاختش is a past tense verb 'placed/set' (بنشاخت) with the object clitic ‑ش 'him' attached; the verb sequence thus means 'placed him upon'.
segments.s625-g11: بدو is the prepositional clitic 'to him' (به + او contracted). گفت is the verb 'said' (the provided senses are nominal; the intended reading is the verbal past 'he said'). کایدر is an archaic/interrogative adverb ('why/what here' or 'why here'); بماندی is second-person past from ماندن 'you remained/you stayed' (past form with final ‑ی), تو is the emphatic pronoun 'you', and دیر is the adverb 'long/for a long time' (i.e. 'so long').
segments.s625-g12: ز is the poetic preposition 'from', دیدار is 'seeing/meeting', این is the demonstrative 'this', مرز is 'border/frontier/region', ناگشته is the negative participle 'not having become/untouched by', and سیر is the noun/adjective 'sated/filled' (so ناگشته سیر = 'not made sated' or 'not satisfied').
segments.s625-g13: This line's object 'مرا' is tokenized here as م + را (m + ra) — meaning 'me'. رزم خاقان is a genitive phrase 'the khaghan's battle/warfare' (رزم = battle, خاقان = khagan/ruler). ز تو is the prepositional phrase 'from you'. باز داشت is a compound verb meaning 'kept back / prevented / held back' (باز conveying the 'back/away' sense and داشت the past auxiliary/main verb). The clause as a whole means 'the khaghan's campaign kept me from you.'
segments.s625-g14: به گیتی is a locative ('in/throughout the world'), followed by مرا ('me' again, here tokenized as م + را). همچو is the simile 'like/as', انباز is an archaic/obscure noun (left untranslated as an old technical term), and داشت is the past verb 'had/kept'. The clause literally says 'in the world he kept me like an‑baz.'
segments.s625-g15: کنون is the temporal adverb 'now'. روزگار here carries the sense 'fortune/fate' (poetic use). توام functions as a bound/poetic relational form tied to 'تو' (expressing 'your' or 'as regards you' in this phrase), تازه is the adjective 'fresh/new', and شد is the past 'became' — together 'now your fortunes became renewed.'
segments.s625-g16: ترا بودن is a verbal-nominal phrase 'your being / your staying' (ترا = 'you' with object/possessive function; بودن = verbal noun 'being'). ایدر (اینجا) is the adverb 'here', بی‌اندازه is the adjective/adverb 'excessive/without measure', and شد is 'became' — 'your being here became excessive.'

sentences.s625-s57: And from there the wise, skilled mobed came proudly to Badr.
sentences.s625-s58: The renowned Caesar’s envoy left the shah’s presence and went toward the house.
sentences.s625-s29: Thus the mobad said to the wise man, "Hurry, and do not turn away from the path of knowledge."
sentences.s625-s30: To me, the answer to what you said is one; the matter, inwardly and outwardly, is slight.
sentences.s625-s31: Outside is the sky, and within it is the air.
sentences.s625-s32: That same boundless one in the world is God, if you can gain radiance through knowledge.
sentences.s625-s45: You will always hold him in contempt — that is the very turning of fate for him.
sentences.s625-s46: Whoever sees someone struck by a thrown arrow stands amazed — therein remains a swift ruin.
sentences.s625-s47: The stars count from the heavens — 'What is more despicable than this, O joyful one?'
sentences.s625-s48: I know this: if there is any answer besides this, the Creator's counsel is broad.
sentences.s625-s25: To the mobad he said thus: "O guide, what is that which you are reciting within?"
sentences.s625-s26: The other — the one you call outside — apart from this you also do not know his name.
sentences.s625-s27: What is 'above,' O chief, and what is 'below'? Who is that boundless one, and who is the lowly?
sentences.s625-s28: What was that thing whose name was widespread? He had authority over me in every place.
sentences.s625-s9: Whatever you say we will answer, and by your voice we will make (it) a happy/fortunate day.
sentences.s625-s10: The old messenger cried out in praise, saying, 'May time and the earth not be without you.'
sentences.s625-s11: Those kings who possess wisdom gained peace from the words of the wise.
sentences.s625-s12: The closer (one is) to the wise God, the darker the ill‑wisher's day.
sentences.s625-s13: You are superior to the chiefs of the world; indeed you are at once a chief, a king, and the better one.
sentences.s625-s14: You are endowed with knowledge and wit and justice, and with farr (royal glory), above the customs of triumphant kings.
sentences.s625-s15: That very wisdom is yours, and pure judgment — among the wise you are the headman.
sentences.s625-s16: May your body and soul be everlasting and sound; may the heavens not show you weak among men.
sentences.s625-s49: When Caesar's spokesman heard the reply, he kissed the ground and issued a decree.
sentences.s625-s50: "To Bahram he said, 'O sovereign king, do not seek increase (superiority) over God.'"
sentences.s625-s51: For the whole world is by your command; the heads of the rebellious are under your covenant.
sentences.s625-s52: The approval of the great, well-born ones — the world has no remembrance of a king like you.
sentences.s625-s21: The king said to him, “Say these words: give the speaker more reputation.”
sentences.s625-s22: He commanded that the mobed become the chief of mobeds, and that he come forward in the presence of the elders and notables.
sentences.s625-s23: The mobed was made, and whoever was learned they (the rulers) made capable in every kind of knowledge.
sentences.s625-s24: The speaker revealed the secret from concealment; he told the emperor’s words to the mobed.
sentences.s625-s53: Moreover, your instruction from the mobeds is greater in learning than that of the wise.
sentences.s625-s54: All the philosophers are his servants; before his wisdom they are put to shame.
sentences.s625-s55: When Bahram heard (it), he rejoiced; brightness increased within his heart.
sentences.s625-s56: He gave the mobed a dirham; to Badr (as well) he gave the same robe and steed and many things.
sentences.s625-s33: Above is like paradise and below is hell; woe to him who is bold against God.
sentences.s625-s34: Another, whose fame was great, wandered about; wherever he went his desire was fulfilled.
sentences.s625-s35: Wisdom exists, O elder; great renown comes from wisdom, and wisdom brings the king to his desire.
sentences.s625-s36: One they call 'Mehr' and another 'Vafa'; wisdom departed, and pain and cruelty remained.
sentences.s625-s1: The spahbed summoned the messenger and seated that famed man before his court.
sentences.s625-s2: When the alert king of the world heard, he bade the messenger come before the guests.
sentences.s625-s3: Came the worldly‑wise, learned old man — eloquent, learned, and a keen learner.
sentences.s625-s4: Having stretched out his hand and humbled and lowly, he sat down on the royal throne on his knees.
sentences.s625-s37: He called eloquence 'truth'; he knew the cunning of the high‑rising star.
sentences.s625-s38: Sometimes forbearing and sometimes a keeper of secrets, so that words remain safe in his presence.
sentences.s625-s39: The renown of wisdom is widespread; his name surpasses all bounds.
sentences.s625-s40: Do not suppose there is anything higher than wisdom; wisdom is head over all good things.
sentences.s625-s17: Your tongue is a balance, and your speaking is jewel by jewel; a jewel is hard indeed to be equaled by gold.
sentences.s625-s18: Although I am Caesar's messenger, still I am that same servant — I am the king's servant.
sentences.s625-s19: I bring greetings from Caesar to the king, that this head, crown, and throne may be eternal.
sentences.s625-s20: And furthermore he ordered that I ask seven things of your learned men as well.
sentences.s625-s41: Wisdom seeks the world's abundant secrets that the eye of the head does not see, (for they are) hidden.
sentences.s625-s42: Another (one) — he whom the world-ruler holds contemptible — in every knowledge concerning the Maker's deeds.
sentences.s625-s43: A star gleams from the high heavens, so that a seeing one may know its count — how many.
sentences.s625-s44: The high heavens are so lofty that there is not even a farsang to them; for no one has a way or access to them.
sentences.s625-s5: They asked Bahram, and he struck him; he set him upon the victorious throne.
sentences.s625-s6: He said to him, 'Why do you remain here so long? From seeing this border you have not grown sated.'
sentences.s625-s7: The khaghan's warfare kept me away from you; in the world he held me like an anbāz (an obscure/archaic term).
sentences.s625-s8: Now your fortunes have been renewed; your being here has become excessive.
